title: Examining Scaling and Transfer of Language Model Architectures for Machine Translation

structured.summary: The paper examines the performance of language model (LM) architectures in machine translation tasks and compares them with Encoder-Decoder models across various scaling and data conditions. The findings suggest that although LMs can potentially match or exceed the quality of Encoder-Decoder models, they lag in computational efficiency.

strengths.point: Comprehensive exploration of LM architectural choices.
strengths.evidence: The paper investigates several design choices like causal masking, full-visible masking, and source language modeling objectives, comparing them with Encoder-Decoder models across different scales and tasks (Section 1).
It highlights that different LMs display unique scaling properties and that architectural differences diminish as model size increases (Abstract).

strengths.point: Significant zero-shot translation improvements by LMs.
strengths.evidence: LMs with full-visible masking outperform Encoder-Decoder models in zero-shot translation tasks by reducing off-target translations (Abstract).

weaknesses.evidence: The paper primarily conducts a comparative analysis using existing LM architectures without introducing new methods or architectural innovations (Section 3).
The paper lacks detailed empirical evidence on how specific factors like dataset size and model parameters affect scaling properties (Sections 1 and 2).

weaknesses.point: Limited Dataset and Language Pair Scope.
weaknesses.evidence: The paper does not specify the range of datasets or language pairs used for evaluation, limiting the generalizability of its conclusions (Abstract, Section 1).
There is no detailed analysis on how different languages might uniquely benefit from architectural choices (Abstract, Section 3).

weaknesses.point: Overlooking the Understanding of Zero-Shot Mechanisms.
weaknesses.evidence: The mechanisms behind the noted zero-shot improvements are not explored in depth (Abstract, Conclusion).

weaknesses.point: Lack of Clarity on Evaluation Metrics.
weaknesses.evidence: The paper does not clearly define which metrics are used for performance evaluation, leaving the assessment criteria ambiguous throughout the methodology and results discussion (Sections 1 and Results).

weaknesses.point: No Consideration for Practicality in Real-world Applications.
weaknesses.evidence: There is a lack of discussion on how the increased computational inefficiency of LM architectures might affect their deployment in real-world scenarios (Abstract, Section 3).
The paper lacks detailed information on the experimental setup, including hyperparameters and training schedules, affecting reproducibility (Section 3).
There is insufficient examination of negative transference issues and their impact in multilingual settings (Section 2).
The paper compares various LM architectural variants but lacks in-depth insight into their specific operational differences within the experiments (Section 3).

weaknesses.point: Lack of Analysis on Training Dynamics.
weaknesses.evidence: There is no detailed analysis of training dynamics such as convergence behavior or training time per model architecture (Abstract and Results).
The paper lacks robust definitions of practical experimental setups and their compliance with industry translation tasks (Section 3).

questions.question: What datasets and language pairs were utilized for evaluation?
questions.motivation: Clarifying the dataset and language scope is essential for assessing the generalizability and applicability of the results.

questions.question: What specific evaluation metrics were used to measure translation quality?
questions.motivation: Understanding the evaluation metrics is crucial for interpreting the performance claims and comparing them with other studies.

questions.question: How do the noted architectural differences impact the computational costs in practical applications?
questions.motivation: Exploring the computational implications is important for real-world deployment considerations.

critique_points.point_001: Lack of Novelty in Methodology.. The paper primarily conducts a comparative analysis using existing LM architectures without introducing new methods or architectural innovations (Section 3)
critique_points.point_002: Insufficient Examination of Scaling Laws.. The paper lacks detailed empirical evidence on how specific factors like dataset size and model parameters affect scaling properties (Sections 1 and 2)
critique_points.point_003: Limited Dataset and Language Pair Scope.. The paper does not specify the range of datasets or language pairs used for evaluation, limiting the generalizability of its conclusions (Abstract, Section 1)
critique_points.point_004: Underexplored Language-Specific Effects.. There is no detailed analysis on how different languages might uniquely benefit from architectural choices (Abstract, Section 3)
critique_points.point_005: Overlooking the Understanding of Zero-Shot Mechanisms.. The mechanisms behind the noted zero-shot improvements are not explored in depth (Abstract, Conclusion)

critique_points.point_007: No Consideration for Practicality in Real-world Applications.. There is a lack of discussion on how the increased computational inefficiency of LM architectures might affect their deployment in real-world scenarios (Abstract, Section 3)
critique_points.point_008: Reproducibility Concerns.. The paper lacks detailed information on the experimental setup, including hyperparameters and training schedules, affecting reproducibility (Section 3)
critique_points.point_009: Inadequate Discussion on Negative Transference.. There is insufficient examination of negative transference issues and their impact in multilingual settings (Section 2)
critique_points.point_010: Insufficient Details on Architectural Comparison.. The paper compares various LM architectural variants but lacks in-depth insight into their specific operational differences within the experiments (Section 3)
critique_points.point_011: Lack of Analysis on Training Dynamics.. There is no detailed analysis of training dynamics such as convergence behavior or training time per model architecture (Abstract and Results)
critique_points.point_012: Generalizability and Practical Setup Noncompliance.. The paper lacks robust definitions of practical experimental setups and their compliance with industry translation tasks (Section 3)
